**Xerez Club Deportivo, S.A.D.**[[1]](#cite_note-RAE-1) is a Spanish [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [Jerez de la Frontera](/wiki/Jerez_de_la_Frontera "Jerez de la Frontera"). Founded in 1947, it currently plays in Group 4 of the [Segunda Federación](/wiki/Segunda_Federaci%C3%B3n "Segunda Federación"), the fourth tier of the Spanish football league system.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

When the [Kingdom of Castile](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Castile "Kingdom of Castile") took Jerez from the Muslims on 9 October 1264 during the period known as the [Reconquista](/wiki/Reconquista "Reconquista"), the city was renamed *Xerez*, representing the phoneme /ʃ/ (which eventually evolved into the current /x/) with the letter *x*. Shortly thereafter, *de la Frontera* ("of the Frontier") was added as it was located on the [Frontier with the Kingdom of Granada](/wiki/Granada_War "Granada War") and was a frequent scene of skirmishes between the two kingdoms. More than two centuries later, after the conquest of Granada in 1492, Xerez lost its status as a frontier city definitively, but it did not lose the name.

The first football club in the city, the Jerez Football Club, was followed by the Xerez Fútbol Club, which recovered the classic spelling *x* instead of the modern *j*; however, the pronunciation remains the same (/x/ in Spanish, similar to a strong 'h'). The Jerez Club Deportivo, a direct descendant of the former, decided to pay homage to it at the request of former president Pablo Porro Guerrero, through two measures. The first was changing the shirt to the one used by the first club, switching from vertical blue and white stripes to a plain blue shirt. This kit is the current one and only differs from that of Xerez Fútbol Club in that the latter used black socks. The second measure was the recovery of the spelling *x*, becoming Xerez Club Deportivo. On 29 September 1992, the club was transformed into a [Sociedad Anónima Deportiva](/wiki/Sociedad_An%C3%B3nima_Deportiva "Sociedad Anónima Deportiva") (Public Limited Sports Company) and adopted its current definitive name: *Xerez Club Deportivo Sociedad Anónima Deportiva*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Chapin-XerezCD-00472.jpg/250px-Chapin-XerezCD-00472.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chapin-XerezCD-00472.jpg)

Stand at [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"), 2007–08 season.

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

Due to the link that [Jerez](/wiki/Jerez_de_la_Frontera "Jerez de la Frontera") established with the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") via the export of its [Sherry](/wiki/Sherry "Sherry") wine to [England](/wiki/England "England"), football began to be practiced informally in the city very early on.[[2]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_4-2) The first written record of football in Jerez dates from 1 November 1870 and appeared in the newspaper "El Progreso"; it reads: *"We know that today a game of Cricket will be played, in the immediate site of the hippodrome, whose spectacle will begin at twelve o'clock sharp in the morning. In the afternoon, the fans of bumps will enjoy a while of foot-ball".*[[3]](#cite_note-3) Other early notes appearing in Jerez publications about football date from 1876 and 1884. The first one reads *"new sport of strange rules and complicated foreign words"*, in the second note it reads *"amusements of employees of English export firms based in Jerez fond of the goal"*.[[2]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_4-2)

In subsequent years there is talk of a *football* society in Jerez, but due to the little importance the new sport had, not many references are made to it. The clearest allusion is from 1887, when a news item appears reporting that *"the Jerez society"* will pay for the travel of some English sailors from the [Port of Cadiz](/w/index.php?title=Port_of_Cadiz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Port of Cadiz (page does not exist)") to Jerez to play a friendly match.[[2]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_4-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Jerez_Cricket_%26_Football.png/250px-Jerez_Cricket_%26_Football.png)](/wiki/File:Jerez_Cricket_%26_Football.png)

Newspaper El Progreso, 1 November 1870.

### Xerez Fútbol Club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Xerez Fútbol Club")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Placa_Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20180918_195753_990.jpg/250px-Placa_Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20180918_195753_990.jpg)](/wiki/File:Placa_Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20180918_195753_990.jpg)

Plaque in honor of Sir Thomas Spencer.

A few years later, the great introducer of football in Jerez appeared, [Sir](/wiki/Sir "Sir") Thomas Spencer, an employee of the Bodega William & Humbert. He was the creator, president, player, and captain of the "Sociedad Jerez Foot-Ball Club". This was the first society of which there are clear references in Jerez and dates from 1907. It started with a simple white kit,[[2]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_4-2) but soon changed to blue and white colors. Other early Jerez teams were "Fortuna Foot-Ball Club" (yellow and black stripes) in 1908, which was later absorbed by Sociedad Jerez; "Jerez Balompié" (blue and claret stripes) in 1912, and "España-Mundo Nuevo F.C." (red and white stripes) in 1913. Other teams referenced in newspapers of the time are "Jerez Bote Club", "Racing Jerezano", "Club Deportivo Hércules de Jerez" and "Jerez Sporting Club", but due to their short lives, there are not many references to them, except isolated mentions.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_5-5)

Despite this proliferation of teams, Sociedad Jerez Foot-Ball Club was the most important team in the city and, due to the diverse ways of referring to its nomenclature in the newspapers of the time, it is named as Jerez, Xerez, Xerez F.C., Jerez F.C. or Xerez Club. The merger with "Fortuna F.C." soon arrived and its activity was intermittent. It is doubted if it was created even before the year 1907 and the Jerez society alluded to in 1887 in a match with "English sailors" was this same society.[[6]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_2-6)[[5]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_5-5)

One of the most important re-foundations the team had was in December 1933,[[6]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_2-6) with José Manuel Domecq Rivero as president. The Villa Mercedes football field was designated as the home ground, the construction of the [Estadio Domecq](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Domecq&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Domecq (page does not exist)") was announced, and this time the official name was Xerez Fútbol Club.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The most important milestones of this team were playing in the promotion phase to [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") twice and reaching the quarter-finals of the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") (1943), where they were eliminated by [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid").[[5]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_5-5)

### Foundation of Xerez Club Deportivo and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Foundation of Xerez Club Deportivo and early years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Placa_Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20200712_114712_018.jpg/250px-Placa_Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20200712_114712_018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Placa_Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20200712_114712_018.jpg)

Plaque in tribute to the Foundation of Xerez CD.

On 4 June 1942, Club Deportivo Jerez was constituted as a reserve team of Xerez Fútbol Club. Both clubs shared facilities and stadium. Highlights of that era include consecutive [Pichichi trophies](/wiki/Pichichi_Trophy "Pichichi Trophy") in the second division in 1943/44 and 1944/45 by [Pato Araujo](/w/index.php?title=Pato_Araujo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pato Araujo (page does not exist)"), with the team close to being promoted to the first division.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Due to a combination of poor sporting and economic results, the disappearance of Xerez Fútbol Club was announced on 26 August 1946, and the board reorganized leading the reserve team Club Deportivo Jerez which remained as an independent team. On 24 September 1947, the club was refounded with the name "Jerez Club Deportivo".[[9]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_1-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) On 21 August 1963, the name was changed to "Xerez Club Deportivo" in honor of the original Xerez Fútbol Club, which in turn refers to the name of the city after the Christian reconquest.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The current club took, scrupulously and contrary to most football clubs that ignore new re-foundations,[[9]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_1-9) the date of September 1947 as its founding date. In reality, it could have taken any of the previous important dates as the founding date, since the history of football in Jerez since its arrival has been a gradual transfer through different people taking over.[[6]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_2-6)[[9]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_1-9) If the sports society had made the same decision as most football clubs, it could have taken 1876 as the founding date (because the "Football Society" of Jerez already existed then), 1907 or 1911 with the foundation of "Sociedad Jerez Foot-Ball Club", 1914 for the merger with "Fortuna Foot-Ball Club", 1922 for the first reappearance of Jerez FC, 1933 for its second reappearance,[[12]](#cite_note-Guia_Marca-12) or 1946 when CD Jerez was intervened.[[6]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_2-6)[[5]](#cite_note-ref_duplicada_5-5)

#### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1950s")]

This is one of the golden decades of *Xerecismo*. After the impossibility of promotion to [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División") in the 40s due to the "high national interest" of the dictatorial regime, which decided to promote [España de Tánger](/w/index.php?title=Uni%C3%B3n_Deportiva_Espa%C3%B1a&action=edit&redlink=1 "Unión Deportiva España (page does not exist)"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) the club was promoted and allowed to play for the first time in [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División") (1953–1954), where it managed to remain for five consecutive years. Notable signings included Rafa Verdú (later a prominent figure in the club's management[[14]](#cite_note-14)). The first official Jerez derby was played between Jerez CD and Jerez Industrial (1958).

Also noteworthy is the creation of the [Trofeo de la Vendimia](/w/index.php?title=Trofeo_de_la_Vendimia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trofeo de la Vendimia (page does not exist)"), in whose first edition (1952) [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") defeated Jerez C.D. 6–3.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Two [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B") championships were achieved.

#### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1960s")]

It was difficult for Jerez to regain the second division status despite dominating the third division in this decade, winning two championships and four runner-up spots; only in 1967 did it have a fleeting stint in the silver category of Spanish football, remaining for a single season.

The name was changed from Jerez C.D. to Xerez C.D.

#### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1970s")]

Another Third Division championship was achieved, leading the team back to the second division, but the joy lasted only one season. In the 1973–1974 season, they were close to the relegation places, which would have been a major setback. The division system was remodeled and Xerez moved to Segunda B.

#### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1980s")]

Two [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B") championships were won, both rewarded with promotion to [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"). The first was again an ephemeral passage, but the second, achieved with a goal by [Antonio Poyatos](/wiki/Antonio_Poyatos "Antonio Poyatos"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) was the beginning of a golden era of 5 years where at times they fought with teams at the top of the table.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Xerez signed a black player for the first time in its history, "El Pantera" Benítez.

In 1988, the mythical [Estadio Domecq](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Domecq&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Domecq (page does not exist)") was left behind to move to play at [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"), which was inaugurated in a match against [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

#### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1990s")]

It was hard for Xerez to get out of [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B"), although in a historic season with local players, in 1996/97, one of the most awaited promotions was achieved against [Gramanet](/wiki/UDA_Gramenet "UDA Gramenet"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) which only served to remain for one season in [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División").

In 1992 Xerez became a [Sociedad Anónima Deportiva](/wiki/Sociedad_An%C3%B3nima_Deportiva "Sociedad Anónima Deportiva") (Public Limited Sports Company), so the club ceased to belong to the members and passed into the hands of shareholders.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In the 1994–95 season, the first victory at the [Estadio Ramón de Carranza](/wiki/Estadio_Ram%C3%B3n_de_Carranza "Estadio Ramón de Carranza") was achieved, with a goal by Javi Peña.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1997, businessman [Luis Oliver](/w/index.php?title=Luis_Oliver_Albesa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luis Oliver Albesa (page does not exist)") took over the presidency of the club, promoting the team from [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B") to [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), although his initially good relations with the City Council deteriorated.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### 21st Century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 21st Century")]

#### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2000s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Chapin-XerezCD-00454.jpg/250px-Chapin-XerezCD-00454.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chapin-XerezCD-00454.jpg)

Pitch and hotel.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Racing-Xerez_2005_012.jpg/250px-Racing-Xerez_2005_012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Racing-Xerez_2005_012.jpg)

Match between [Racing de Ferrol](/wiki/Racing_de_Ferrol "Racing de Ferrol") and Xerez CD played in 2005.

With the year 2001 came a new promotion of Xerez to [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), consummated with a 2–0 victory against [CD Toledo](/wiki/CD_Toledo "CD Toledo") at the [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"), a success that caused a huge [pitch invasion](/wiki/Pitch_invasion "Pitch invasion") by the fans.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) A new stage opened for the Xerez entity, which from the first moment would seek success in the silver division of Spanish football.

On the other hand, on the extra-sporting level, the City Council denounced [Luis Oliver](/w/index.php?title=Luis_Oliver_Albesa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luis Oliver Albesa (page does not exist)"), majority shareholder and president of the entity.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

During the [2001–02 season](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2001–02 Segunda División"), the first after its return to the category, Xerez was very close to achieving promotion to [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), with [Bernd Schuster](/wiki/Bernd_Schuster "Bernd Schuster") as coach. However, the club's precarious economic situation, which kept the players unpaid and forced the team to play many home matches in [Sanlúcar de Barrameda](/wiki/Sanl%C3%BAcar_de_Barrameda "Sanlúcar de Barrameda"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) decisively influenced a resounding fall in the last 8 matchdays, in which they were only able to get 4 points, wasting a very important cushion of points with respect to the fourth classified. Finally, on 19 May 2002, Xerez lost 2–1 against [Recreativo de Huelva](/wiki/Recreativo_de_Huelva "Recreativo de Huelva"), coached by [Lucas Alcaraz](/wiki/Lucas_Alcaraz "Lucas Alcaraz"), saying goodbye definitively to promotion.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Elche-Xerez.jpg/250px-Elche-Xerez.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elche-Xerez.jpg)

Match against [Elche CF](/wiki/Elche_CF "Elche CF").

In subsequent seasons, it established itself as one of the constant contenders for promotion and imposing itself as one of the strongest teams in the championship (despite remaining in economic hardships[[29]](#cite_note-29) that even forced the players to appear semi-naked in a magazine as a sign of protest). In the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2005–06 Segunda División"), coached by [Lucas Alcaraz](/wiki/Lucas_Alcaraz "Lucas Alcaraz"), they managed to be winter champions only to fall into an inexplicable negative streak and finish seventh in the League. A similar situation was experienced in the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2006–07 Segunda División"), with [Pepe Murcia](/wiki/Pepe_Murcia "Pepe Murcia") on the bench, where the team occupied promotion places between the 3rd and 17th matchdays, but deflated again in the second round and finished 9th.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Viqueira-XerezCD-p1040379.jpg/250px-Viqueira-XerezCD-p1040379.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viqueira-XerezCD-p1040379.jpg)

Emilio Viqueira.

During the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2007–08 Segunda División"), Xerez remained in the relegation zone for 28 matchdays. On 12 January 2008, after a resounding defeat against [UD Las Palmas](/wiki/UD_Las_Palmas "UD Las Palmas") (0–3) and 10 games without winning, the president and majority shareholder Joaquín Morales presented his resignation in a press conference,[[30]](#cite_note-30) which ultimately did not become effective.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The coach, [Juan Martínez Casuco](/w/index.php?title=Juan_Mart%C3%ADnez_Casuco&action=edit&redlink=1 "Juan Martínez Casuco (page does not exist)") (who had previously replaced the resigned [Miguel Ángel Rondán](/w/index.php?title=Miguel_%C3%81ngel_Rond%C3%A1n&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miguel Ángel Rondán (page does not exist)")),[[32]](#cite_note-Casuco,_nuevo_técnico_del_Xerez-32) was sacked after losing to [Numancia](/wiki/CD_Numancia "CD Numancia") (0–3) in the 24th matchday.[[33]](#cite_note-El_Xerez_anuncia_la_destitución_de_Casuco_como_entrenador-33) His successor was [Esteban Vigo](/wiki/Esteban_Vigo "Esteban Vigo")[[34]](#cite_note-Esteban_vuelve-34) who, despite taking charge of a team that seemed doomed and starting with 3 consecutive defeats, managed to save Xerez from relegation in the last matchday, after achieving 8 consecutive home victories (a historical club record).[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) That season, the Xerez striker [Yordi](/wiki/Yordi "Yordi") won the [Pichichi Trophy](/wiki/Pichichi_Trophy "Pichichi Trophy")[[37]](#cite_note-37) and [Zarra Trophy](/wiki/Zarra_Trophy "Zarra Trophy") of the Segunda División, with 20 goals scored, 9 of them from [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_kick "Penalty kick").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Ascenso1-Bus2.JPG/250px-Ascenso1-Bus2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ascenso1-Bus2.JPG)

Promotion celebration.

On 19 June 2008, the majority shareholder and president, Joaquín Morales, announced in a press conference the sale of his shares to Francisco Garrido, a businessman born in [Espera](/wiki/Espera "Espera") but based in [Jerez de la Frontera](/wiki/Jerez_de_la_Frontera "Jerez de la Frontera").[[38]](#cite_note-38) However, the sale did not materialize, and Morales began negotiating the sale with Sevillian businessman Francisco Nuchera. The agreement became unofficial on 12 September 2008, although Xerez did not release details due to the lack of signatures before a [notary](/wiki/Notary "Notary").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)
The [Mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") of [Jerez de la Frontera](/wiki/Jerez_de_la_Frontera "Jerez de la Frontera"), [Pilar Sánchez](/wiki/Pilar_S%C3%A1nchez "Pilar Sánchez"), announced that the City Council would continue to support the club.[[41]](#cite_note-41) This operation ultimately did not happen, with Joaquín Morales remaining as the maximum shareholder.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Alex_Berganti%C3%B1os_Real_Club_Deportivo_de_la_Coru%C3%B1a.jpg/250px-Alex_Berganti%C3%B1os_Real_Club_Deportivo_de_la_Coru%C3%B1a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Berganti%C3%B1os_Real_Club_Deportivo_de_la_Coru%C3%B1a.jpg)

[Álex Bergantiños](/wiki/%C3%81lex_Berganti%C3%B1os "Álex Bergantiños") was a starter in the historic promotion to La Liga in the 2008–09 season.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

After this "in extremis" salvation, in the [2008/09 season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2008–09 Segunda División"), after an excellent start,[[44]](#cite_note-44) Xerez surprised everyone by finishing the first half as winter champions and finding themselves in promotion places to [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") as category leaders. Some professionals described the team as one of the best squads in the club's history.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

On 19 March 2009, youth coordinator and former goalkeeper Carlos Osma became the new President, replacing Joaquín Bilbao, who resigned after being involved in an altercation for which he had to testify before a judge, after which he was released without charges.[[46]](#cite_note-El_presidente_del_Xerez_renuncia_a_su_puesto_tras_quedar_en_libertad_sin_cargos-46)
On 30 May 2009, the team led by [Esteban Vigo](/wiki/Esteban_Vigo "Esteban Vigo"), after a spectacular season, practically sealed promotion to [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") after beating [SD Eibar](/wiki/SD_Eibar "SD Eibar") 3–0, which was mathematically achieved on 13 June by beating [SD Huesca](/wiki/SD_Huesca "SD Huesca") at home 2–1.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Additionally, in the last matchday, the team managed to finish as champions of [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División").[[49]](#cite_note-49) It was undoubtedly a totally unexpected success, as the Andalusian team had barely achieved permanence in the previous season and faced a significant lack of signings before starting the new campaign.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

However, shortly after, bad news arrived for the Xerez fans: [Esteban Vigo](/wiki/Esteban_Vigo "Esteban Vigo"), the coach considered the main architect of the promotion,[[51]](#cite_note-51) did not receive a renewal proposal from the board[[52]](#cite_note-52) and left for [Hércules CF](/wiki/H%C3%A9rcules_CF "Hércules CF").[[53]](#cite_note-53) This provoked protests and incidents against the majority shareholder, Joaquín Morales.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/42_segundos_%283940204405%29.jpg/250px-42_segundos_%283940204405%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:42_segundos_(3940204405).jpg)

[La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") match at [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid")'s stadium.

On 15 July 2009, the [Fundación Xerez Club Deportivo](/w/index.php?title=Fundaci%C3%B3n_Xerez_Club_Deportivo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fundación Xerez Club Deportivo (page does not exist)") was registered in Seville, independent of the S.A.D., with objectives including social goals such as the dissemination of sporting values in youth through academy management, dissemination of football history in Jerez, and management of future sports infrastructure.[[55]](#cite_note-Fundación-55) The foundation's board had eleven trustees distributed among the club, City Council, Provincial Council, veterans, and fan clubs.[[55]](#cite_note-Fundación-55) The first President of the Foundation was Juan Carlos González Benítez.

#### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2010s")]

In January 2010, coach [José Ángel Ziganda](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_%C3%81ngel_Ziganda "José Ángel Ziganda") was dismissed due to poor results in the debut in [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") (only 7 points in 18 matches), preceding the arrival of a new owner, although [Antonio Poyatos](/wiki/Antonio_Poyatos "Antonio Poyatos") had to act as interim coach for one match.[[56]](#cite_note-El_Xerez_destituye_al_'Cuco'_Ziganda-56) However, after a turbulent end to 2009 off the pitch, an Argentine business group led by Federico Souza acquired the majority of shares from Joaquín Morales,[[57]](#cite_note-Federico_Souza-57) on 15 January 2010. The chosen coach was [Néstor Gorosito](/wiki/N%C3%A9stor_Gorosito "Néstor Gorosito"),[[58]](#cite_note-Gorosito_dirigirá_al_Xerez_español-58) who managed to change the team's face and start a positive streak.

However, despite staying in the fight for permanence until the last matchday, the team finally sealed its relegation to [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División") after drawing 1–1 against [CA Osasuna](/wiki/CA_Osasuna "CA Osasuna"),[[59]](#cite_note-59) as Xerez needed to win and rely on other results.

With the return to [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División"), the club entered a new phase, directed by bankruptcy administrators, with [Emilio Viqueira](/wiki/Emilio_Viqueira "Emilio Viqueira") as sports director and [Javi López](/wiki/Javi_L%C3%B3pez_(footballer,_born_1964) "Javi López (footballer, born 1964)") as coach.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-El_reto_de_Javi_López_es_devolver_al_Xerez_a_Primera-62) Key players left, replaced by veterans like [Pablo Redondo](/wiki/Pablo_Redondo "Pablo Redondo"), [Barber](/wiki/Rafa_Barber "Rafa Barber"), [José Mari](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mari_(footballer,_born_1978) "José Mari (footballer, born 1978)") and [Gerard Autet](/wiki/Gerard_Autet "Gerard Autet").[[63]](#cite_note-63)

On 23 February 2011, a new Board was formed with [Antonio Millán Garrido](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Mill%C3%A1n_Garrido&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antonio Millán Garrido (page does not exist)") as president. The 2010–11 season ended in 8th place. For the 2011–12 season, [Juan Merino](/wiki/Juan_Merino "Juan Merino") was hired but was dismissed after 16 matchdays, replaced by [Vicente Moreno](/wiki/Vicente_Moreno "Vicente Moreno").[[64]](#cite_note-Vicente_Moreno_seguirá_siendo_el_entrenador_del_Xerez-64)

In early 2012, debt reached almost 29 million euros.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Jesús Gómez Martos bought shares but left shortly after. The team saved itself from relegation.

In July 2012, the bankruptcy process concluded, returning power to Joaquín Morales. For the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2012–13 Segunda División"), [Esteban Vigo](/wiki/Esteban_Vigo "Esteban Vigo") returned, but after a terrible run, he was dismissed.[[66]](#cite_note-Esteban_destituido-66) His replacement, [Carlos Ríos](/wiki/Carlos_R%C3%ADos "Carlos Ríos"), could not prevent relegation to [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B"), ending a 12-year professional spell.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

On 28 June 2013, [Xerez Deportivo FC](/wiki/Xerez_Deportivo_FC "Xerez Deportivo FC") was born by fans to replace Xerez CD, which was on the verge of dissolution. On 1 August 2013, Xerez CD was administratively relegated to [Tercera División](/wiki/Tercera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Tercera División") due to debts with players.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The club also lost the use of Chapín stadium and had to play in various grounds in the province (La Granja, Rota, etc.).

After several seasons of instability, relegation to regional leagues (1ª Andaluza), and financial struggles, the club managed to return to Tercera División in 2017.

In October 2017, Ricardo García and the Energy group left, and a group involving [Luis Oliver Albesa](/w/index.php?title=Luis_Oliver_Albesa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luis Oliver Albesa (page does not exist)") and [Afición Xerecista](/w/index.php?title=Afici%C3%B3n_Xerecista&action=edit&redlink=1 "Afición Xerecista (page does not exist)") took over management.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

On 16 January 2019, the club exited the judicial liquidation process.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In the 2019–20 season, debts almost prevented the team from competing, but fundraising allowed them to play.

#### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2020s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Exposici%C3%B3n_75_aniversario_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20221004_124413.jpg/250px-Exposici%C3%B3n_75_aniversario_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20221004_124413.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exposici%C3%B3n_75_aniversario_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20221004_124413.jpg)

75th Anniversary Exhibition

In 2022, the club celebrated its 75th anniversary with a match against [Cádiz CF](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz_CF "Cádiz CF").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In the 2023–24 season, the team achieved the feat of being the only team in the big five leagues not to concede a goal after 9 matchdays.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In matchday 33, after beating [CD Gerena](/wiki/CD_Gerena "CD Gerena") 2–0, Xerez CD secured promotion to [Segunda Federación](/wiki/Segunda_Federaci%C3%B3n "Segunda Federación") virtually.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In the 2024–25 season, Xerez Club Deportivo secured permanence[[74]](#cite_note-74) in the category several matchdays before the end of the championship, which kept their chances of playing the promotion *play-off* to [Primera Federación](/wiki/Primera_Federaci%C3%B3n "Primera Federación") alive. However, these possibilities faded after dropping points against teams from the lower zone, such as [CD Don Benito](/wiki/CD_Don_Benito "CD Don Benito") and [CF Villanovense](/wiki/CF_Villanovense "CF Villanovense"). In matchday 33, after the defeat against [Xerez DFC](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_DFC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xerez DFC (page does not exist)"), coach Checa announced that he would not continue leading the team the following season[[75]](#cite_note-75).

For the 2025–26 season, Xerez Club Deportivo incorporated [Diego Galiano](/w/index.php?title=Diego_Galiano_Jim%C3%A9nez&action=edit&redlink=1 "Diego Galiano Jiménez (page does not exist)") as coach, a technician from Jerez with extensive experience in [Segunda Federación](/wiki/Segunda_Federaci%C3%B3n "Segunda Federación"), recognized for his experience at [Atlético Antoniano](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Antoniano "Atlético Antoniano").

On 27 June, Xerez CD was the protagonist of prominent news[[76]](#cite_note-76) following the publication of the annual list of debtors of the [Spanish Tax Agency](/wiki/Spanish_Tax_Agency "Spanish Tax Agency"). Although the information was officially disseminated by the club that same day, it had already been advanced hours earlier by the tax body. According to this list, the blue entity had achieved a substantial reduction of its debt with the Treasury, which went from exceeding **eight million** euros in the previous year to standing slightly above **two million** in 2025, representing a decrease of approximately **85%**. This improvement in the club's economic situation could be further accentuated in the next fiscal year, as part of the pending debt would be **subject to prescription**, which could reduce the amount owed to just **400 euros**.

## Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Symbols")]

### Shield

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Shield")]

The shield of Xerez Club Deportivo was designed during the summer of 1947 by Mariano Aricha de Vicente.[[77]](#cite_note-escudo-77)

The shield is clearly inspired by the coat of arms of the city of [Jerez de la Frontera](/wiki/Jerez_de_la_Frontera "Jerez de la Frontera"), incorporating the same colors. It is divided into two parts by a white diagonal strip that spans from the top right to the bottom left corner. In the upper left part is the central motif of the city's coat of arms, the blue waves on a silver (white) background. In the lower right corner, the letters [C](/wiki/C "C") and [D](/wiki/D "D") are intertwined in white on a blue background. The shield is crowned, like the city's coat of arms, this time with a five-point crown.

Inside the diagonal strip, "XEREZ" can be read in blue. Originally it was designed with the word "JEREZ". The "J" was replaced by an "X" due to the initiative of president Pablo Porro Guerrero, who on 21 August 1963 decided to recover the word "Xerez" to refer to the club from which it proceeds, the Xerez Fútbol Club.[[77]](#cite_note-escudo-77)

The shield received a second modification in the year 2000, where the tone of the color was changed, making it a little softer and with less saturation. This minor aesthetic change was made so that the shield would stand out more on the dark blue of the uniform. Occasionally, the shield is usually depicted outside the kit with the original darker tone.

On 31 May 2017, it was announced that the patent of the shield, as well as the name "Xerez Club Deportivo", became part of [Afición Xerecista](/w/index.php?title=Afici%C3%B3n_Xerecista&action=edit&redlink=1 "Afición Xerecista (page does not exist)").[[78]](#cite_note-78)

In September 2020, for a new sports project, the club's design team decided to restyle the shield, eliminating the black outlines of its predecessor, with a cleaner and more current look. It was also adapted to different versions (positive and negative).

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Chap%C3%ADn_-_Jerez_-_IMG_20180728_212044_367.jpg/250px-Chap%C3%ADn_-_Jerez_-_IMG_20180728_212044_367.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chap%C3%ADn_-_Jerez_-_IMG_20180728_212044_367.jpg)

[Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Campojuventud1.jpg/250px-Campojuventud1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campojuventud1.jpg)

[Estadio de la Juventud](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_de_la_Juventud&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio de la Juventud (page does not exist)").

Xerez Club Deportivo played its first matches in the old [Estadio Domecq](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Domecq&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Domecq (page does not exist)"), which it shared with [Jerez Industrial CF](/wiki/Jerez_Industrial_CF "Jerez Industrial CF"). In 1988, the club moved to the newly built [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"), which was inaugurated with a match against [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid").

From the 2013–14 season, and as a consequence of the application of the Regulatory Ordinance for Carrying out Activities in Municipal Sports Facilities, the club encountered difficulties in using the city's municipal facilities. This situation forced the team to live a period of sporting exile, training and competing in various stadiums in the province. During that period, Xerez played matches in Jerez fields such as [La Juventud](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Pedro_S._Garrido&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Pedro S. Garrido (page does not exist)"), La Canaleja, La Granja, El Torno and Picadueñas, among others.

The 2017–18 season was especially unstable, with the club acting as home team in up to seven different stadiums: [Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"), [La Juventud](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Pedro_S._Garrido&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Pedro S. Garrido (page does not exist)"), [El Palmar](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_El_Palmar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio El Palmar (page does not exist)") ([Sanlúcar de Barrameda](/wiki/Sanl%C3%BAcar_de_Barrameda "Sanlúcar de Barrameda")), Antonio Fernández Marchán ([Guadalcacín](/w/index.php?title=Guadalcac%C3%ADn_(Jerez_de_la_Frontera)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Guadalcacín (Jerez de la Frontera) (page does not exist)")), Andrés Chacón ([La Barca de la Florida](/w/index.php?title=La_Barca_de_la_Florida&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Barca de la Florida (page does not exist)")), Municipal Sánchez Portella ([Torrecera](/w/index.php?title=Torrecera&action=edit&redlink=1 "Torrecera (page does not exist)")) and Municipal de El Torno ([El Torno](/wiki/El_Torno "El Torno")).

In 2021, Xerez returned permanently to the [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"), where it currently plays thanks to the notable growth of its social mass and the institutional strengthening of the club.

### Chapín

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Chapín")]

Main article: [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín")

From 1988 to 2014, Xerez played its home games at the [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín"). The stadium was inaugurated in 1988, with a capacity for 20,523 spectators. Its dimensions are 105x68 meters.

One of the stands with the most public attendance was Preferencia. The Tribuna was frequented by well-known people in Jerez. The presidential box, VIP boxes, and broadcasting booths are also located there. The area with the least attendance was Fondo Norte. The fourth stand is Fondo Sur, which was not numbered at the request of the fans, as it was this stand that had the most attendance and atmosphere. This multipurpose stadium is often criticized for the difficulty of vision for spectators due to the separation distance between the stands and the grass, because of the athletics tracks.

After several years playing in different stadiums in the province due to municipal restrictions, Xerez Club Deportivo returned permanently to the [Estadio Municipal de Chapín](/wiki/Estadio_Municipal_de_Chap%C3%ADn "Estadio Municipal de Chapín") in 2021.

### Other stadiums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Other stadiums")]

Until 1988, the team played at [Estadio Domecq](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Domecq&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Domecq (page does not exist)").
During the [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2001–02 Segunda División") season, while Chapín was being remodeled for the [2002 FEI World Equestrian Games](/wiki/2002_FEI_World_Equestrian_Games "2002 FEI World Equestrian Games"), Xerez played at "[Estadio El Palmar](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_El_Palmar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio El Palmar (page does not exist)")" in [Sanlúcar de Barrameda](/wiki/Sanl%C3%BAcar_de_Barrameda "Sanlúcar de Barrameda") or "Bahía Sur" in [San Fernando](/wiki/San_Fernando,_C%C3%A1diz "San Fernando, Cádiz"). Finally, it returned to Jerez to play first at the remodeled [Estadio de la Juventud](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_de_la_Juventud&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio de la Juventud (page does not exist)") and finally at Chapín.

## Uniform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Uniform")]

In its early years, Xerez wore a blue shirt and white shorts. Blue, the main color since its foundation, reflects the link with the city of Jerez de la Frontera.
Since the 2022–23 season, Xerez wears kits from the German brand [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas").

* **Home**: Blue shirt with crew neck, white sleeves and white details on the sides. Shoulders with the three Adidas stripes in blue.
* **Away**: Black shirt with a white diagonal sash crossing the chest from left to right, gold crew neck.
* **Third**: Orange shirt with watermark diagonal line pattern, black shoulders and V-neck.
* **Goalkeeper**: Long-sleeved shirt in fluorescent yellow.

## Youth System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Youth System")]

At the end of the 2014/15 season and in order to reduce expenses due to the bad economic situation of the club, Xerez abolished most of its youth system, keeping only a Youth team. Among the teams that disappeared was [Xerez B](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_Club_Deportivo_%22B%22&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xerez Club Deportivo \"B\" (page does not exist)"), which had been relegated that same season. At the same time, an affiliation agreement was announced with [Xerez Balompié](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_Balompi%C3%A9&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xerez Balompié (page does not exist)").

Since the 2017/18 season, several teams belonging to the association [Afición Xerecista CD](/w/index.php?title=Afici%C3%B3n_Xerecista_CD&action=edit&redlink=1 "Afición Xerecista CD (page does not exist)") act as the youth academy of Xerez Club Deportivo. For the 2019/20 season, the intention to increase the number of academy teams with four new teams: school (baby), benjamin, alevín and infantile, as well as a senior women's team and a futsal section, was announced.

In the 2023/24 season, Xerez Club Deportivo communicated that the entity would manage its own grassroots football teams, approving the new strategic plan called "Cantera Xerez CD". Similarly, the team will maintain the affiliation agreement with Afición Xerez CD for greater enrichment, with the idea of re-enhancing Jerez grassroots football.

## Trofeo de la Vendimia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_CD&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Trofeo de la Vendimia")]

Main article: [Trofeo de la vendimia](/w/index.php?title=Trofeo_de_la_vendimia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trofeo de la vendimia (page does not exist)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Exposici%C3%B3n_75_aniversario_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20221004_124155.jpg/250px-Exposici%C3%B3n_75_aniversario_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20221004_124155.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exposici%C3%B3n_75_aniversario_Xerez_CD_-_IMG_20221004_124155.jpg)

Trophy

This trophy was played for the first time on 7 September 1952, a match played in the old [Estadio Domecq](/w/index.php?title=Estadio_Domecq&action=edit&redlink=1 "Estadio Domecq (page does not exist)") against [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"). Since then, many high-level teams have disputed this trophy, which was lost in subsequent seasons. After the [Trofeo Teresa Herrera](/wiki/Trofeo_Teresa_Herrera "Trofeo Teresa Herrera") in [A Coruña](/wiki/A_Coru%C3%B1a "A Coruña"), it is the oldest held in the entire Spanish geography.

From 2014, [Xerez DFC](/w/index.php?title=Xerez_DFC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xerez DFC (page does not exist)") took over the rights to it by registering its patent. In 2015 Xerez CD began celebrating the "Trofeo Rafa Verdú" in recognition of its Honorary President and later in 2020 Xerez C.D presented the [Trofeo Feria de la Vendimia](/w/index.php?title=Trofeo_Feria_de_la_Vendimia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trofeo Feria de la Vendimia (page does not exist)").
